claims.subtopic: RBI Monetary Policy and Surplus Management
claims.sectionTitle: RBI Monetary Policy: Quantitative and Qualitative Tools
claims.claimText: Under the Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR), banks are required to keep a percentage of their Net Demand and Time Liabilities (NDTL) as cash with the RBI and do not earn any interest on it.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: CRR is the percentage of NDTL banks must keep as cash with RBI, earning no interest.
claims.examUse: Statement-based question testing the features of CRR, specifically whether it earns interest or can be kept in non-cash formats.
distractorHooks: Banks earn a nominal interest rate on CRR deposits with the RBI.
CRR can be maintained in the form of government securities or gold.

claims.subtopic: RBI Monetary Policy and Surplus Management
claims.sectionTitle: RBI Monetary Policy: Quantitative and Qualitative Tools
claims.claimText: The Statutory Liquidity Ratio (SLR) mandates banks to maintain a percentage of their NDTL in liquid assets, which can include gold, bonds, or cash.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: SLR is the percentage of NDTL banks must maintain in liquid assets like gold, bonds, or cash.
claims.examUse: Correct-pair or statement-based question comparing the eligible assets for SLR versus CRR.
distractorHooks: SLR balances must be maintained exclusively in cash.
SLR balances must be deposited directly with the RBI.

claims.subtopic: RBI Monetary Policy and Surplus Management
claims.sectionTitle: RBI Monetary Policy: Quantitative and Qualitative Tools
claims.claimText: Quantitative monetary tools directly impact the overall volume of money supply, whereas qualitative tools selectively influence the direction of credit flow to specific sectors.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: QUANTITATIVE TOOLS (Volume of Credit) - Directly impact the overall money supply... QUALITATIVE TOOLS (Direction of Credit) - Selectively influence credit flow to specific sectors
claims.examUse: Classification question asking candidates to distinguish between the objectives and examples of quantitative vs. qualitative tools.
distractorHooks: Qualitative tools are designed to control the overall volume of money supply in the economy.
Margin requirements and moral suasion are examples of quantitative monetary tools.

claims.subtopic: RBI Monetary Policy and Surplus Management
claims.sectionTitle: RBI Monetary Policy: Quantitative and Qualitative Tools
claims.claimText: An increase in the Repo Rate, which is the short-term lending rate of the RBI to banks, leads to a decrease in the overall money supply.
claims.evidenceExcerpt: Repo Rate is the rate at which RBI lends short-term to banks; increasing it decreases money supply.
claims.examUse: Cause-and-effect question on how changes in policy rates affect macroeconomic variables like money supply and inflation.
distractorHooks: Increasing the Repo Rate increases the money supply in the economy.
The Repo Rate is the rate at which the RBI lends long-term funds to commercial banks.
